attributes.preview:
# MBD4 Loss of Function: Treatment Vulnerability Evidence Brief

**Version:** 2.0 (plain-English tumor board edition)
**Date:** 2026-05-15
**Source analysis:** DepMap 24Q2 · GDSC2 pharmacological screens · published isogenic and clinical data
**Scope:** Pan-cancer cell line analysis with ovarian cancer context noted where directly supported
**Research Use Only.** These findings are computational and preclinical. They are not intended to guide diagnosis, prognosis, or treatment selection outside of a research setting.

---

## Section 1 — Clinical Question

**When a tumor carries a loss-of-function mutation in MBD4, which treatments are most likely to exploit that specific genetic defect?**

This brief evaluates four candidate treatment strategies — cytidine analog chemotherapy, ATR inhibitors, PARP inhibitors, and immune checkpoint inhibitors — and assigns each a confidence level based on the available evidence.

---

## Section 2 — Bottom-Line Answer

Read this first. The rest of the brief explains the evidence behind each statement.

| Treatment | Confidence level | One-line summary |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Cytidine analogs (gemcitabine, cytarabine) | **Validated in lab models** | Strongest evidence. MBD4-deficient cells are ~10× more sensitive. Confirmed in isogenic models, animal models, and one patient case. |
| ATR inhibitors (ceralasertib) | **Strong computational candidate — not yet validated in patients** | MBD4-deficient cell lines are significantly more sensitive. Signal held across four independent checks. Needs isogenic and clinical confirmation. |
| PARP inhibitors | **One proposed mechanism not supported — class not ruled out** | The specific reason PARP inhibitors were expected to work (MBD4 loss causing cells to make more PARP1 protein) was not seen in the data. PARP inhibitors may still have a role, but not through this mechanism. |
| Immune checkpoint inhibitors | **Hypothesis only — early signals in one cancer type**
